Discover how SAP AI is applied across Finance, Supply Chain, CX, and consulting scenarios. Through live demonstrations and practical examples, learn how to identify high-value use cases and activate embedded AI capabilities across SAP solutions. 

  • Day 3: Build & Experiment  

Dive into the technical side of SAP AI with sessions on Joule Studio, Generative AI Hub, Document AI, AI Agents, MCP servers, and SAP's latest AI innovations. Hands-on exercises and SAP CodeJam experiences provide opportunities to build, extend, and experiment with AI solutions. 

  • Day 4: Trust & Scale   

Explore SAP Joule architecture, multi-agent systems, governance, security, and operating models needed to scale AI successfully across the enterprise. 

Sessions are designed for business users, developers, consultants, enterprise architects, technical teams, and partners supporting AI adoption initiatives.  

Pre-registration is open now. Sign up to be notified when individual sessions open for registration.

entry.title: My First DCom Experience: Innovation, Collaboration, and a Glimpse into the Future
entry.content:
Recently, I had the opportunity to participate in the Bay Area Developer Community (DCom) event for the first time, and it was an experience that left a lasting impression on me.  Going into the event, I knew DCom would be an opportunity to learn about innovative projects happening across SAP. What I did not fully appreciate was the energy, creativity, and passion that would be on display throughout the day. Walking through the event and speaking with teams from different organizations, I was reminded of how much innovation is happening across SAP and how many talented people are working together to shape the future of enterprise software.

One of the things that stood out most was the diversity of ideas being presented. Every booth had a unique story to tell.  The event themes centered around SAP's Autonomous Enterprise vision, Joule, Agentic AI, SAP Business Suite transformation, and the evolving role of intelligent systems in business processes. Rather than hearing about these concepts in presentations or strategy discussions, DCom provided an opportunity to see them come to life through real projects and working demonstrations.  I had the privilege of participating as part of a joint effort between the CloudOps and Deployment Hub teams. Together, we hosted a booth that showcased how intelligent agents and conversational experiences can simplify customer onboarding activities. While I was proud of the demonstration itself, what I enjoyed most was the opportunity to engage with attendees, exchange ideas, and learn how other teams are approaching similar challenges.

The conversations throughout the day were often as valuable as the demonstrations. Attendees asked thoughtful questions, shared their own experiences, and offered different perspectives on where AI and automation can create meaningful impact. Those discussions reinforced the importance of collaboration and reminded me that innovation rarely happens in isolation.  Another aspect of the event that I appreciated was the strong sense of partnership across teams. Our demonstration was the result of close collaboration between CloudOps and Deployment Hub, bringing together expertise from different areas to solve a common problem. As I walked around the event, I saw many similar examples of teams working across organizational boundaries to build something larger than any single group could accomplish on its own.  For me, DCom was also a reminder that technology is ultimately about people. It's easy to focus on architectures, platforms, and features, but the most memorable part of the event was meeting colleagues who are passionate about solving problems, helping customers, and pushing the boundaries of what is possible.

By the end of the day, I left feeling energized and optimistic about where we are headed. The event provided a glimpse into how SAP's strategic vision is being translated into practical solutions by teams across the company. It also reinforced the importance of curiosity, collaboration, and continuous learning in driving innovation.  My first DCom experience was both inspiring and rewarding. I had the opportunity to showcase work that I am proud of, connect with talented colleagues, and learn about exciting initiatives happening across SAP. Most importantly, I left with a greater appreciation for the people and ideas that are helping shape the future of our company.

I am already looking forward to next year's event.

entry.title: SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Library and New Enhancements
entry.content:
This blog is meant to be a another quick read. The idea is to introduce the SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Library and the latest SAP Joule for Consultants enhancements over your morning coffee ☕ or tea 🍵, and show how pre-built Expert templates, SAP Expert Library, smoother conversations, and peer-to-peer Expert sharing can support SAP transformation work in a more practical way.

In the first blog, we explored how the SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace can combine SAP public knowledge, consulting experience, customer-specific assets, and governance logic into a personalized digital Expert. The latest release takes the next step by introducing the SAP Expert Library as a structured entry point: it helps users create Experts faster, work with Joule for Consultants more efficiently, and reuse expert knowledge across a project team.

There are three themes in this release that immediately stood out to me: the SAP Expert Library with SAP pre-built Expert templates, a more flexible conversation experience, and direct Expert sharing with colleagues. Let us take a closer look.

Figure 1: The SAP Expert Library provides both starter templates and SAP pre-built Expert templates.

 

SAP Expert Library: from a blank page to an SAP-guided starting point

Creating a high-quality Expert is not only about writing a prompt. A useful consulting Expert needs a clearly defined role, scope, goals, guardrails, decision logic, output structure, language settings, and deliverables. Building all of this from the beginning can take time, especially for a domain-specific expert.

The SAP Expert Library reduces that initial effort through SAP pre-built Expert templates. These templates provide a ready-made structure that users can review, adapt, and enrich instead of starting with an empty configuration.

A template can include:

  • Predefined expert goals and working scope
  • Recommended personas and guardrails
  • Standardized output formats and context instructions
  • Language settings and global deliverables
  • Additional recommended configuration elements

This does not turn the Expert into a fixed black box. The template is a starting point. Users remain in control and can modify the fields according to their project, organization, and delivery needs.

 

The first wave in the SAP Expert Library: four domain-specific templates

In the first release wave, the SAP Expert Library in SAP Joule for Consultants provides four domain-specific templates covering important consulting and migration scenarios:

Logistics Expert

Assess / Build

Supports SAP S/4HANA conversion topics in logistics, including Simplification Items, consistency errors, compatibility scope, prerequisite SAP Notes, and SAP Readiness Check preparation.

Manufacturing Expert

Design / Build

Supports design, configuration, implementation, troubleshooting, and optimization across discrete, process, and repetitive manufacturing scenarios using SAP S/4HANA and related solutions.

Clean Core Expert

Design / Governance

Provides concise and actionable guidance aligned with SAP Clean Core principles, helping project teams make architecture and governance decisions more consistently.

Custom Code Expert

Design / Build

Focuses on ABAP custom code analysis, SAP S/4HANA readiness, ATC findings, Simplification Database analysis, technical remediation, and SAP Clean Core compliance.

Figure 2: The first wave includes Logistics, Clean Core, Manufacturing, and Custom Code Experts.

The value is not simply that four names appear in the SAP Expert Library. Each template already carries domain language, an expected working scope, and a recommended consulting perspective. This gives users a more meaningful starting point and helps teams avoid reinventing the same instruction structure again and again.

 

SAP best-practice structure plus customer context

A pre-built template becomes much more powerful when it is combined with customer-specific information. Users can upload attachment files to help the Expert understand the customer's business processes, terminology, project documents, internal standards, and delivery expectations.

Typical attachments could include:

  • Customer-specific process documentation
  • Project standards and internal guidelines
  • Best practice files and supporting materials
  • Governance rules, checklists, templates, and lessons learned

This creates a practical knowledge-fusion model: SAP provides a structured and domain-aware foundation, while the customer provides the context that makes the Expert relevant for a real project. The result can be both SAP best-practice-based and tailored to the organization.

 

How to create an Expert from the SAP Expert Library

Like the other features of SAP Joule for Consultants, getting started with the SAP Expert Library is easy through a guided flow:

  1. Open the Expert Workspace in the SAP Joule for Consultants Console.
  2. Go to the SAP Expert Library and select the SAP Experts tab.
  3. Choose the domain template that best matches your use case and click Apply Template.
  4. Review and modify the Expert name, description, tags, custom instructions, guardrails, output expectations, and other predefined fields.
  5. Upload supporting attachments when additional context is needed.
  6. Click Create to generate your personalized Expert.

Figure 3: Select a template, apply it, and review the preconfigured Expert content.

Figure 4: Add supporting attachments when needed, then create the personalized Expert.

Note:

1. The important step is number four. Do not treat the template as something that must be accepted without review. Read the goals, persona, core principles, responsibilities, and guardrails. Then adapt them to the project scope. A strong template accelerates the design process, but good consulting judgment still matters.

2. The current SAP Expert Library is a first version. As a user of Joule for Consultants, you can expect more features to be added gradually, making the expert more flexible, more personalized, and more versatile.

Once the Expert is created from the SAP Expert Library, it appears in My Experts and can be used in a conversation just like an Expert created from scratch. The difference is that the initial design effort is reduced and the starting configuration is more consistent.

Happy testing!

 

A smoother experience for everyday consulting conversations

The release is not only about the Expert Workspace. Several user-experience improvements also make day-to-day interaction with SAP Joule for Consultants more intuitive and flexible.

A fresh new welcome screen

The welcome page now has a fresh new look that makes it easier to get started and navigate key capabilities. The prompt field remains central, while Quick Links and frequently used entry points help users move directly into common tasks.

Figure 5: The refreshed welcome screen provides clearer access to prompts and key capabilities.

 

Up to 50 active conversations

Users can now keep up to 50 active conversations at once, increased from the previous limit of 10. This is a practical improvement for consultants who frequently switch between projects, workstreams, or technical topics and need to preserve context across parallel discussions.

Figure 6: SAP Joule for Consultants now supports up to 50 active conversations.

Retry an answer without starting over

When a response is not quite right, users no longer need to rephrase the entire question from scratch. Select Retry, explain what should change. For example, make the answer shorter, more detailed, or focused on another aspect, and ask it to generate an improved response.

Figure 7: The Retry action supports faster refinement of an answer.

 

Edit the original prompt

Users can also edit a prompt after it has been sent. This is useful when a question contains a mistake, lacks important context, or needs a more precise scope. Instead of repeating the whole interaction, edit the original prompt and continue from the corrected version.

Figure 8: A sent prompt can now be edited directly.

 

Share an Expert directly with colleagues

Another important enhancement is peer-to-peer Expert sharing. An Expert can now be shared directly with colleagues who use the same SAP Joule for Consultants tenant. This helps teams reuse working instructions and domain expertise without asking an administrator to distribute each Expert.

The sharing flow is straightforward:

  1.   Open an Expert that you created and click Share.
  2.   Enter your colleague's work email address and press Enter.
  3.   Click Add to include the colleague in the sharing list.
  4.   The recipient opens the Shared With Me tab in the Expert Workspace.
  5.   The recipient selects the shared Expert and adds it to My Experts.

 

After adding the shared Expert to My Experts, the recipient can modify it. These changes apply only to the recipient's own copy and do not affect the original Expert created by the colleague.

A few things to keep in mind

Sharing works only between users in the same SAP Joule for Consultants tenant (the same tenant URL).

Only the Expert instructions are shared. Attached files are not included.

Each recipient receives an independent copy, while the original Expert remains unchanged.

No administrator action is required.

From a knowledge-management perspective, this is a meaningful step. A well-designed Expert from the SAP Expert Library can move from an individual productivity tool to a reusable team asset, while every recipient still has the freedom to tailor the copy for a specific role or project.

 

Why these updates matter for SAP migration and transformation projects

Taken together, the new capabilities improve much more than the setup experience.

Faster onboarding: The SAP Expert Library and its pre-built templates help new users learn from a structured domain-specific example instead of facing a blank instruction field.

Greater consistency: Teams can start from common domain logic, terminology, guardrails, and output expectations.

More room for parallel work: Up to 50 active conversations make it easier to keep separate client, project, and topic contexts available.

Faster iteration: Retry and prompt editing reduce repetition and help users refine the direction of a conversation more efficiently.

Reusable team knowledge: Peer-to-peer sharing allows proven Expert instructions to be reused by colleagues as independent copies.

In other words, this release helps shift the conversation from 'How do I set everything up?' to 'How do I use and adapt this capability for the real decisions in my project?' That is a much more valuable place for SAP consultants and key users to spend their time.

 

To summarize

  1. The SAP Expert Library provides SAP pre-built Expert templates as a structured starting point for domain-specific consulting Experts.
  2. The first template wave in the SAP Expert Library includes Logistics, Manufacturing, Clean Core, and Custom Code Experts.
  3. Customer attachments can add project-specific context to a template-based Expert.
  4. The refreshed welcome screen makes key capabilities easier to access.
  5. Users can keep up to 50 active conversations and refine work with Retry and prompt editing.
  6. Experts can be shared with colleagues in the same tenant as independent copies; attached files are not included.
  7. Together, these enhancements can reduce setup effort, improve consistency, and turn expert knowledge to a more reusable team asset.

Of course, a single feature is not enough. The real combination comes from using the SAP Expert Library, customer knowledge, project governance, efficient conversations, and team sharing together with the broader capabilities of SAP Joule for Consultants.

Related reading

Blogs:

  • Getting Started with SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace
  • Enhance SAP Joule for Consultants with Custom Knowledge Grounding
  • SAP Joule for Consultants Application & benefits in Implementation projects

SAP Help:

  • Onboarding Guide for SAP Joule for Consultants
  • SAP Joule for Consultants
  • SAP Joule for Consultants Console
  • SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace

Now you have probably finished your coffee or tea 🍵. I hope this quick overview has given you a clear taste of how the SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Library and the latest enhancements can make Expert creation, daily conversations, and team collaboration more practical for real migration and transformation work.

I am truly impressed by how quickly SAP Joule for Consultants continues to add useful capabilities. I cannot wait to explore deeper use cases for the SAP Expert Library, the new templates, conversation features, and much more new features in future blogs! 🎇

Let's keep moving forward on the SAP transformation journey by learning, improving, and building smarter together! 🚀

entry.title: How to Connect Claude Code to ABAP with Zero-Config MCP
entry.content:
Before We Start - What This Post Deliberately Skips
- What is Claude Code, and what are the available models?
- What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and how it works conceptually?
- What is vibe coding and why AI-assisted development matters?
- What are the benefits of using AI in SAP development?
- What development platforms are available (Web, Desktop, CLI)?
If you need more details, you can find these topics in sap community portal. Read that, then come back here.

What This Post Covers - and Why It Matters?

This post walks you through connecting Claude Code to a live SAP system using the ABAP Remote Filesystem extension's native MCP server - no external Node.js setup, no manual JSON editing, just a single toggle. 

What this changes for SAP development:

  • No more hand-typed routine code. TYPE declarations, SELECT statements, ALV setup - written directly into the SAP object from a single prompt.
  • No more AI guesswork. Without a live connection, AI tools hallucinate tables and function modules that don't exist. With MCP, every suggestion is grounded in your actual system.
  • No more setup overhead. What used to require a custom Node.js project now takes one checkbox and one command - open to any developer with VS Code.
  • Clean Core by default. Claude works through the same ADT API and released objects that Eclipse uses, so generated code fits your governance model instead of fighting it.

This is the point where AI-assisted ABAP stops being a chat-window experiment and starts being a real part of your IDE.

 
Goal: I will cover the entire topic in 4 parts:
|Part 1| Installing Claude Code on Windows and setting up the VS Code extension |
|Part 2| Connecting VS Code to your live SAP system via the ABAP FS extension |
|Part 3| Enabling the native MCP server with a single toggle - no Node.js setup, no JSON editing |
|Part 4| A live demo: one plain-English prompt creates a real, running ABAP report on SAP |
If you're here, you already know why this matters. Let's get to the actual steps.
 
Pre-requisite: This guide assumes you already have an active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise). If not, sign up at claude.ai before proceeding
 
Part 1 - The Setup: Getting Claude Code Running on Windows
Step 1.1 - Install Claude Code CLI
Open PowerShell (the built-in Windows command-line shell) and run:

 

This downloads Anthropic's official installer and executes it in one step. 'irm' fetches the script, 'iex' runs it.
> ⚠️ Common Error - Git Bash Not Found
> Claude Code needs a bash shell to run on Windows. If you see this error, install Git for Windows from [git-scm.com/downloads/win](https://git-scm.com/downloads/win). During setup, select: Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software. Then re-run the install command above.
Step 1.2 — Verify the Installation

 

Close PowerShell and open a fresh window. Run:
You should see a version number like 'claude 1.x.x'. If PowerShell says claude is not recognized, the PATH wasn't set automatically. Fix it manually:

 

1. Start → search Environment Variables in system settings
2. Edit the environment variables for your account
3. Select Path → Edit → New → paste ( in this case path can be taken from powershell commnad prompt)
 for eg: 

 

4. Close and reopen PowerShell, then re-run claude --version.

> Run claude doctor - this command checks your PATH, authentication, Node.js version, and config in one shot. Always the first step when something isn't working.
Step 1.3 - Launch Claude Code and the VS Code Extension
Navigate to your project folder and launch Claude Code.
The first launch opens your browser to authenticate with your Anthropic account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise all work). 
For everyday ABAP work, the VS Code extension is where you'll spend most of your time.
Download and Install VS code from code.visualstudio.com for windows if you don't already have it. Once it is installed.Open VS Code, press 'Ctrl+Shift+X', search for Claude Code (published by Anthropic), and install it. Once installed, the Claude icon appears in the Activity Bar and connects automatically to your account.

 

The extension gives you a persistent chat panel inside VS Code - ask Claude about the file you have open, highlight any ABAP block and ask it to explain or refactor, and switch between ABAP and CAP projects in the same session. No terminal switching required.
> Note on Software Center installs: If your organization restricts installations, Claude Code for VS Code is typically available via your company Software Center / Company Portal.
 
Part 2 - Connecting VS Code to Your SAP System via ABAP FS
This is where the magic begins. The ABAP Remote Filesystem (ABAP FS)extension - built by Marcello Urbani - connects VS Code to your SAP system using the same ADT REST API that Eclipse uses. Once connected, your ABAP objects (programs, classes, CDS views, behavior definitions) appear as files in VS Code. Claude Code can then read, write, and review them like any local code file.
Pre-requisites Before You Connect
You'll need these four things from your SAP system before starting:
| Hostname or IP | The address of your SAP server (hostname preferred - IP causes SSL issues explained below) |
| ADT Service active | Go to transaction SICF → '/sap/bc/adt' must be activated |
| Client number| e.g. '100' - visible in the SAP logon screen |
|Username & Password| Your regular SAP development user |
| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: Building an Enterprise AI Architecture on SAP: Joule, BTP AI Core, and the Data Foundation
entry.content:
This blog covers the architectural layers required for enterprise-grade AI in SAP landscapes — from the data foundation through AI services to governance. It is intended for solution architects, enterprise architects, and technical leads designing AI capability on SAP BTP.

Applies to: SAP Joule (embedded across SAP cloud applications as of 2024/2025), SAP BTP AI Core (all available plans), SAP AI Launchpad, SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud. EU AI Act considerations apply to deployments within the European Economic Area.

1. Joule: capabilities, technical architecture, and integration points

Joule is built on SAP's AI Foundation — a layer within BTP that provides the infrastructure for SAP's own AI features and for customer-built AI applications. At a technical level, Joule integrates with:

  • S/4HANA Cloud — via OData APIs and SAP Fiori launchpad integration for natural language ERP interactions
  • SuccessFactors — embedded in Recruiting, Performance & Goals, and Compensation modules
  • SAP Service Cloud — case intelligence, next-best-action recommendations
  • SAP Ariba — sourcing guidance and contract intelligence

Joule activation prerequisites

1. SAP AI Core subscription in your BTP subaccount

Joule depends on SAP AI Core. Ensure the aicore service plan is subscribed in your BTP Global Account entitlements.

2. Trust configuration between BTP and your SAP cloud applications

Joule's cross-application context requires correctly configured identity propagation. Verify your IAS (Identity Authentication Service) tenant is connected to both BTP and your SAP cloud application tenants.

3. Data privacy and residency validation

Confirm that your data processing agreement (DPA) covers Joule's AI model invocations. For EU deployments, verify data residency settings in the SAP AI Core configuration.

4. Role assignment

Assign the Joule_User role collection to the relevant user groups in your BTP subaccount. Business users require no additional technical configuration after role assignment.

2. BTP AI Core: custom model deployment architecture

SAP BTP AI Core aicore provides the managed infrastructure for deploying, serving, and monitoring custom ML models. It uses a resource group model to isolate workloads:

 
BTP AI Core resource group structure

AI Core Instance ├─ Resource Group: finance-forecasting │ ├─ Scenario: demand-forecast-v1 │ ├─ Configuration: model hyperparameters, data connections │ ├─ Training Pipeline: connected to Datasphere dataset │ └─ Deployment: REST endpoint for inference ├─ Resource Group: hr-attrition │ └─ (isolated from finance workloads) └─ Resource Group: supply-chain-anomaly └─ (isolated from all other workloads)

LLM integration via Generative AI Hub

SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub provides governed access to foundation models from multiple providers — including Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o), Anthropic (Claude), Google (Gemini), and Meta (Llama) — through a single, governed endpoint:

 
Generative AI Hub — API call pattern (Python SDK)

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub') llm = ChatOpenAI( proxy_model_name='gpt-4o', proxy_client=proxy_client, max_tokens=1000 ) response = llm.invoke("Summarise the top 5 overdue purchase orders for supplier X")

Data governance note: All prompts sent through Generative AI Hub are governed by your SAP AI Core data processing agreement. Review your DPA before sending personally identifiable information (PII) or commercially sensitive data in prompts. Use data masking or anonymisation where required by your data classification policy.

3. The data foundation: Datasphere, MDG, and BDC

The quality and governance of the data layer determines AI output quality more than the choice of model. Three components form the foundation:

SAP Datasphere — semantic modelling

Datasphere's semantic layer preserves business meaning as data moves from SAP source systems to analytical and AI consumers. Key configuration elements:

  • Spaces — isolated data workspaces per business domain (Finance Space, Supply Chain Space). Each Space has its own data access controls, replication flows, and analytical models.
  • Business Layer objects — Dimensions, Facts, and Analytical Datasets that expose data with business-meaningful column names and relationships
  • Data Access Controls — row-level security applied at the semantic layer, ensuring AI queries respect the same access controls as human users

Master Data Governance (MDG)

AI models trained on or querying data with poor master data quality will produce unreliable outputs. Validate these before any AI initiative:


MDG DomainAI relevanceKey quality check
Customer (BP)Customer segmentation, churn predictionDuplicate rate, address completeness
MaterialDemand forecasting, anomaly detectionClassification consistency, UoM standardisation
Finance (GL)Financial forecasting, anomaly detectionCost centre hierarchy completeness
SupplierRisk scoring, payment predictionBank data completeness, country standardisation

 4. EU AI Act readiness for SAP AI deployments

For deployments within the EEA, the EU AI Act (effective August 2024, obligations phasing in through 2026) introduces requirements for AI systems that interact with business data. Key considerations for SAP AI architectures:

  • Risk classification: Classify each AI use case as Minimal, Limited, High, or Unacceptable risk under the EU AI Act taxonomy. Most SAP operational AI use cases (demand forecasting, anomaly detection) fall under Limited or High risk.
  • High-risk requirements: Human oversight mechanisms, model documentation (technical files), conformity assessment, and registration in the EU AI database
  • Transparency: Users must be informed when they are interacting with an AI system (Joule's disclosure mechanism satisfies this for embedded co-pilot interactions)
  • Data governance: Training data must be documented, bias-checked, and governed — Datasphere's lineage and audit capabilities support this requirement

Summary

  • Joule requires AI Core subscription + IAS trust configuration + DPA validation before activation
  • Use Resource Groups in AI Core to isolate AI workloads by domain
  • Generative AI Hub provides governed multi-model LLM access — review DPA before sending sensitive data
  • Datasphere Spaces + Business Layer objects are the semantic foundation for AI data quality
  • Validate MDG quality in each domain before building AI use cases on that domain's data
  • EU AI Act obligations are phasing in through 2026 — classify your AI use cases now

What AI use cases are you building on SAP BTP? I'm particularly interested in hearing from practitioners who have gone through the Generative AI Hub integration or built custom models on AI Core — what were your biggest technical challenges?

entry.title: AI-Driven SAP Testing: The Next Evolution of SAP Quality Assurance
entry.content:
Technical Friction in Enterprise System Validation

Deploying updates across hybrid SAP landscapes introduces three distinct technical bottlenecks:

  • Complex Customization Maintenance: Custom enhancements (Z-tables, user exits, and custom ABAP developments) often break during SAP upgrades. Traditional automation scripts break with them because minor UI element ID changes or underlying schema updates render static object repositories obsolete.
  • Large-Scale Test Data Management: Validating end-to-end (E2E) transactions requires context-rich, compliant data across interconnected modules (e.g., Order-to-Cash, Procure-to-Pay). Manually provisioning this data creates severe testing bottlenecks.
  • Limited End-to-End Visibility: A standard SAP transaction rarely stays entirely inside SAP; it frequently triggers APIs, middleware (like SAP Integration Suite), and third-party legacy applications. Validating these distributed data flows requires specialized SAP integration testing services rather than simple UI automation.

Strategic Blueprint for AI-Powered Quality Assurance

Transforming an enterprise SAP QA function requires moving away from static test scripts and adopting an adaptive, three-tiered technical framework backed by robust SAP test strategy services:

1. Intelligent Process Discovery

Instead of relying on outdated documentation, AI mining tools analyze SAP transaction logs (ST03N data) and user execution paths. This extracts the actual, real-world business processes directly from the system, creating a highly accurate baseline for the test catalog.

2. Self-Healing Test Automation

When SAP updates modify field IDs, UI properties, or Fiori elements, traditional test scripts fail. SAP test automation services use computer vision and object-recognition algorithms to detect these UI changes in real time. The framework automatically updates the underlying object repository mid-execution, preventing false-positive test failures.

3. Predictive Test Prioritization (Change Impact Analysis)

AI engines parse SAP transport logs to map exactly which transport objects intersect with specific business processes. Instead of running a full suite for a minor hotfix, SAP regression testing services calculate a targeted execution path, running only the scripts directly exposed to the code change.

Performance Matrix: Traditional vs. Intelligent Testing

Validation MetricManual & Legacy AutomationAI-Driven SAP Testing
Defect DetectionLimited to predefined script assertions and manual observation.Proactively identifying edge-case defects via historical run logs and code analysis.
Script MaintenanceManual updates are required whenever a UI element or Fiori component shifts.Algorithmic self-healing resolves object-property changes on the fly.
Test Data PrepManual data staging; high risk of data aging and test degradation.Dynamic data masking, synthesis, and automated state injection.
Regression ScopeRigid, broad-spectrum execution; throttled by time and compute constraints.Dynamic execution scoped directly by change impact analysis.

Operational Advantages of Targeted Validation

Partnering with an experienced SAP testing services provider to deploy modern SAP automated testing tools yields definitive operational improvements:

Faster Release Cycles: By targeting only high-risk modules, regression testing timelines drop from weeks to hours, allowing organizations to keep pace with SAP’s continuous update cycles.

Lower Script Maintenance: Automated self-healing drops script maintenance queues by up to 70%, freeing SAP QA testing services teams from fixing broken selectors to focus on complex functional coverage.

Improved Functional Coverage: SAP functional testing expands past basic SAP screens to validate complex, multi-system workflows (e.g., confirming a Fiori app interaction successfully triggers an external warehouse API and downstream finance ledger entry).

Final Thought

The bottom line is simple. Enterprise SAP landscapes have grown too fast and too complex for teams to test everything manually or maintain fragile, old-school automation scripts. Each new update in the cloud or custom changes brings a risk of disruptive processes.

AI-driven testing solves this by acting as an intelligent filter. It figures out exactly what changed under the hood, heals automation scripts automatically when screens change, and runs only the specific tests that are actually at risk. By turning QA from a massive guessing game into a precise, automated operation, enterprises can safely deploy continuous software updates without risking system downtime or disrupting daily business operations.
